questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman at 33 weeks' gestation is admitted with right upper quadrant pain, nausea, and a blood pressure of 168/104 mmHg. Laboratory studies show platelets 68 x 10^9/L, AST 240 U/L, ALT 210 U/L, and LDH 820 U/L with schistocytes on peripheral smear. She is started on IV magnesium sulfate and antihypertensives. The obstetric and anesthesia teams are consulted regarding next steps. Which of the following is the definitive treatment for this patient's condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: High-dose dexamethasone to reverse the laboratory abnormalities
choices.rationale: Large randomized double-blind trials found no benefit of dexamethasone in improving maternal or perinatal outcomes in HELLP syndrome; corticosteroids do not constitute definitive treatment, though betamethasone may be given for fetal lung maturity before 34 weeks.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Platelet transfusion to a target above 150 x 10^9/L followed by discharge
choices.rationale: Platelet transfusion is reserved for active bleeding or before a procedure when counts are very low; it does not treat the underlying disease, and discharging an unstable preeclamptic patient is unsafe.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Delivery of the fetus and placenta
choices.rationale: Correct. HELLP syndrome is a severe variant of preeclampsia, and the only definitive treatment is delivery of the fetus and placenta, which removes the driver of the disease cascade. Magnesium provides seizure prophylaxis but is not curative.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Continued expectant management with antihypertensives until 39 weeks
choices.rationale: Expectant management to term is inappropriate in HELLP with severe features; ongoing pregnancy risks hepatic rupture, abruption, DIC, and maternal death. Prompt delivery is indicated.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Therapeutic plasma exchange as first-line therapy
choices.rationale: Plasma exchange is considered only for refractory or atypical postpartum cases that fail to resolve after delivery, or when TTP is suspected; it is not the definitive or first-line treatment for HELLP.

questions.explanation: HELLP syndrome (hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, low platelets) is a life-threatening variant of preeclampsia with severe features. Diagnosis rests on hemolysis (elevated LDH, schistocytes, increased bilirubin), AST/ALT at least twice the upper limit of normal, and platelets at or below 100 x 10^9/L. Magnesium sulfate is started for seizure prophylaxis and fetal neuroprotection, and blood pressure is controlled, but these are supportive measures. The only definitive treatment that halts the pathophysiologic cascade is delivery of the fetus and placenta. Corticosteroids do not improve maternal or perinatal outcomes (betamethasone is given separately for fetal lung maturity if before 34 weeks). Anesthetic implications include thrombocytopenia, which may contraindicate neuraxial techniques and necessitate general anesthesia for cesarean delivery.

source.title: HELLP Syndrome - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason: Verified against source https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK560615/ (StatPearls "HELLP Syndrome"), which loads and is public. The vignette is an unambiguous HELLP syndrome presentation at 33 weeks: severe hypertension (168/104), thrombocytopenia (68 x10^9/L), elevated AST/ALT, elevated LDH with schistocytes (hemolysis), RUQ pain, already on magnesium sulfate and antihypertensives. Correct answer C (delivery of the fetus and placenta) is directly supported — the source explicitly states delivery "is the only effective treatment." Distractors are correctly incorrect: (A) the article notes dexamethasone showed no proven benefit on maternal/perinatal outcomes and does not reverse the disease; (B) there is no platelet target of >150 and discharge would be unsafe; (D) expectant management to 39 weeks is inappropriate for severe HELLP at 33 weeks; (E) plasma exchange is not first-line and is reserved for refractory/postpartum or TTP-overlap cases. Medicine, key, and rationales are accurate. Safe to publish to a paid bank.

questions.stem: A 36-year-old woman (G4P3, three prior cesarean deliveries) has antenatal imaging consistent with placenta previa and placenta accreta spectrum. A planned cesarean hysterectomy is scheduled at 35 weeks. During the procedure under initial neuraxial anesthesia, profuse hemorrhage occurs as the surgeons attempt placental separation, and the estimated blood loss rapidly exceeds 2 liters. Which of the following is the most appropriate anesthetic management at this point?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Continue with the existing spinal anesthetic and rely on crystalloid boluses alone
choices.rationale: Crystalloid alone cannot replace ongoing massive blood loss, will worsen dilutional coagulopathy, and the patient is at high risk of becoming hemodynamically unstable and unable to tolerate the neuraxial sympathectomy.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Convert to general anesthesia, secure the airway, and activate the massive transfusion protocol with balanced blood products
choices.rationale: Correct. With massive ongoing hemorrhage, converting to general anesthesia secures the airway and hemodynamics, while activating the massive transfusion protocol delivers packed cells, plasma, platelets, fibrinogen, and tranexamic acid to treat hypovolemia and coagulopathy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Administer a single unit of packed red blood cells and defer further transfusion until laboratory results return
choices.rationale: Waiting for lab results during exsanguinating hemorrhage delays resuscitation; massive transfusion protocols use empiric balanced ratios precisely because point-of-care numbers lag behind clinical blood loss.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Deepen the neuraxial block with additional local anesthetic to improve surgical conditions
choices.rationale: Deepening the block extends the sympathectomy and worsens hypotension in a hemorrhaging patient; it does not address airway control or the need for aggressive blood product resuscitation.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Stop all vasopressors to avoid masking the degree of blood loss
choices.rationale: Vasopressors are needed to support perfusion pressure during resuscitation; withholding them risks profound hypotension and end-organ ischemia and offers no benefit.

questions.explanation: Placenta accreta spectrum, especially with previa and multiple prior cesareans, is a leading cause of catastrophic obstetric hemorrhage and is frequently managed by planned cesarean hysterectomy. Multidisciplinary preparation (obstetrics, anesthesiology, blood bank, urology, interventional radiology), large-bore IV access, and availability of a massive transfusion protocol are essential. While neuraxial anesthesia may be used for a controlled start, the onset of massive hemorrhage warrants prompt conversion to general anesthesia to secure the airway and better manage large-volume resuscitation and vasopressor titration. Resuscitation uses balanced ratios of packed red cells, fresh frozen plasma, and platelets along with fibrinogen and tranexamic acid, started empirically rather than waiting for laboratory confirmation.

source.title: Managing Placenta Accreta and Massive Hemorrhage: A Case Report on Anesthetic and Surgical Interventions - PMC

verdict.reason: URL loads, is public (PMC11304637), and supports the answer. The case report describes anesthetic management of placenta accreta spectrum with cesarean hysterectomy complicated by massive hemorrhage, and explicitly documents (1) conversion from neuraxial to general anesthesia for hemodynamic/situational control, (2) orotracheal intubation/airway securing via video laryngoscope, and (3) massive transfusion protocol activation with balanced blood products (PRBC, FFP, platelets, fibrinogen, tranexamic acid). Medicine is correct and unambiguous: for massive obstetric hemorrhage (>2L) with hemodynamic instability, the standard of care is convert to GA + secure airway + activate MTP with balanced products (option B). All distractors are clearly wrong and dangerous: A (spinal + crystalloid alone) ignores ongoing sympathectomy and inadequate resuscitation; C (single PRBC, defer pending labs) is dangerous undertransfusion; D (deepen neuraxial) worsens hypotension and ignores airway/hemorrhage; E (stop vasopressors) removes needed perfusion support. Rationales are accurate. Safe to publish.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old woman in active labor suddenly develops dyspnea, hypoxia, and hypotension, followed within minutes by cardiac arrest. After return of spontaneous circulation, she develops profuse bleeding from her IV sites and surgical field, and laboratory studies show a markedly prolonged PT/PTT, low fibrinogen, and falling platelets. Which of the following best characterizes the most likely diagnosis and a key management principle?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Amniotic fluid embolism; manage with high-quality resuscitation and aggressive correction of coagulopathy with balanced blood products
choices.rationale: Correct. The abrupt cardiorespiratory collapse during labor followed by severe DIC is classic for amniotic fluid embolism, which produces consumptive coagulopathy in roughly 80% of cases; management is supportive with immediate resuscitation and balanced blood product transfusion.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pulmonary thromboembolism; the priority is immediate full-dose systemic thrombolysis
choices.rationale: The fulminant DIC with active bleeding makes amniotic fluid embolism far more likely than thromboembolism, and full-dose thrombolysis in a hemorrhaging parturient with coagulopathy would be dangerous.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hemorrhagic shock from occult abruption; treat with uterotonics alone
choices.rationale: The presentation began with sudden hypoxia and cardiovascular collapse rather than primary bleeding; uterotonics alone do not address the cardiorespiratory collapse or consumptive coagulopathy of amniotic fluid embolism.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Anaphylaxis to an antibiotic; treat with epinephrine and withhold blood products
choices.rationale: While amniotic fluid embolism shares features with an anaphylactoid response, the accompanying DIC requires aggressive blood product replacement; withholding blood products would be harmful given the active coagulopathic bleeding.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Local anesthetic systemic toxicity; treat primarily with intravenous lipid emulsion
choices.rationale: LAST typically follows a recent local anesthetic bolus and causes CNS and cardiac toxicity without a consumptive coagulopathy; lipid emulsion would not address the DIC and bleeding seen here.

questions.explanation: Amniotic fluid embolism (AFE) is a rare but catastrophic obstetric emergency, often termed the anaphylactoid syndrome of pregnancy. About 70% of cases occur during labor, 19% during cesarean, and 11% after vaginal delivery, and it can occur up to 48 hours after delivery. The classic triad is sudden hypoxia, hypotension/cardiovascular collapse, and coagulopathy, with disseminated intravascular coagulation developing in roughly 80% of patients. There is no specific cure; management is supportive, centered on immediate high-quality cardiopulmonary resuscitation, hemodynamic support with inotropes and vasopressors, and aggressive correction of coagulopathy using balanced blood product resuscitation (packed red cells, plasma, and platelets, with cryoprecipitate/fibrinogen). ECMO has been used for refractory cases. The active coagulopathic bleeding distinguishes AFE from a pure thromboembolic event.

source.title: Amniotic Fluid Embolism - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason: Source https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK559107/ loads, is public (StatPearls "Amniotic Fluid Embolism"), and directly supports the answer. The vignette is a textbook AFE presentation: sudden cardiorespiratory collapse during active labor (hypoxia, hypotension, arrest) followed by DIC (prolonged PT/PTT, low fibrinogen, falling platelets, profuse bleeding). Source confirms AFE = "sudden cardiorespiratory collapse and disseminated intravascular coagulation," coagulopathy in ~80%, and management cornerstones of high-quality CPR/resuscitation plus balanced/massive transfusion (1:1:1) — matching correct answer A. Distractors are correctly wrong: (B) PE doesn't cause consumptive DIC and thrombolysis is contraindicated in a peripartum hemorrhaging patient; (C) abruption + uterotonics alone fails to explain the abrupt hypoxic arrest and ignores coagulopathy; (D) no allergen, anaphylaxis doesn't cause DIC, and withholding blood in active DIC hemorrhage is harmful; (E) no local anesthetic exposure and LAST does not produce low-fibrinogen consumptive coagulopathy. Single best answer, medicine accurate and unambiguous.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old parturient undergoes a category-1 emergency cesarean delivery under general anesthesia. After rapid sequence induction, direct laryngoscopy fails. A second optimized attempt with a different blade and external laryngeal manipulation also fails to achieve intubation, but facemask ventilation is adequate and oxygen saturation is maintained at 96%. Following the Difficult Airway Society/Obstetric Anaesthetists' Association obstetric guidelines, which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Make three further laryngoscopy attempts with the same equipment before changing approach
choices.rationale: The obstetric guideline limits intubation attempts (generally a maximum of two, with a third only by a more experienced operator) to avoid airway trauma and loss of the can-ventilate situation; repeated attempts with the same equipment are not recommended.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Declare failed intubation, insert a second-generation supraglottic airway device, and oxygenate while deciding whether to wake or proceed
choices.rationale: Correct. After failed intubation with adequate oxygenation, the guideline directs declaring failure, placing a second-generation supraglottic airway to maintain oxygenation, and then making a structured decision to wake the patient or proceed with surgery based on the clinical scenario.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Proceed immediately to emergency front-of-neck airway (cricothyroidotomy)
choices.rationale: Front-of-neck access is reserved for the can't-intubate, can't-oxygenate situation. Here ventilation is adequate and saturation maintained, so an invasive airway is not yet indicated.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Release cricoid pressure and allow the patient to breathe spontaneously without any airway device
choices.rationale: Abandoning airway management entirely risks aspiration and hypoxia; the guideline maintains oxygenation with a supraglottic device and a structured plan rather than leaving the airway unsupported.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Administer additional neuromuscular blockade and force a blind nasal intubation
choices.rationale: Blind nasal intubation is not part of the obstetric failed-intubation algorithm and risks bleeding and trauma in an edematous, vascular pregnant airway; oxygenation via a supraglottic airway is preferred.

questions.explanation: Pregnant patients are at increased risk of difficult and failed intubation owing to airway mucosal edema, weight gain, breast enlargement, reduced functional residual capacity, and increased oxygen consumption leading to rapid desaturation. The DAS/OAA obstetric guidelines provide a structured algorithm: optimize the first intubation attempt, limit the number of attempts (generally two, with a third permitted only by a more experienced colleague), and declare failed intubation rather than persisting. With failure but adequate oxygenation, a second-generation supraglottic airway device is inserted to maintain oxygenation, and a deliberate decision is made to wake the patient or continue surgery, weighed against maternal/fetal urgency. Front-of-neck access is reserved for the can't-intubate, can't-oxygenate scenario. Maintaining oxygenation throughout is the overriding priority.

source.title: OAA/DAS Obstetric Airway Guidelines 2015 - Difficult Airway Society

verdict.reason:
Source URL (https://das.uk.com/guidelines/obstetric_airway_guidelines_2015/) loads, is public (no login), and hosts the official DAS/OAA 2015 obstetric difficult/failed intubation guidelines (master algorithm, Algorithms 1-3, Tables 1-2). The medicine is correct and unambiguous. The vignette describes two optimized laryngoscopy attempts failed but adequate facemask ventilation with SpO2 96% — i.e., a "can intubate-fail / can oxygenate" situation, not CICO. Per the guideline pathway (Algorithm 1 -> Algorithm 2 -> Table 1), the correct next step is to declare failed intubation, call for help, insert a second-generation supraglottic airway device to oxygenate, then make the Table 1 "wake or proceed" decision. This is option B, which is verified correct.

Distractor rationales are accurate: A is wrong because the guideline limits laryngoscopy to 2 attempts (max 3 by a senior colleague) and forbids repeated attempts with unchanged equipment; C (immediate eFONA/cricothyroidotomy) is reserved for can't-intubate-can't-oxygenate, which does not apply since ventilation is adequate and SpO2 is maintained; D (release cricoid, no airway device, spontaneous ventilation) is unsafe given aspiration risk after RSI in a parturient and is not the guideline step; E (more NMB + blind nasal intubation) is not part of the algorithm and is unsafe. Answer is unambiguous and well-supported.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman at 38 weeks' gestation with severe rheumatic mitral stenosis presents in early labor. Her obstetrician plans a trial of vaginal delivery. Which of the following anesthetic strategies best matches the hemodynamic goals for this patient during labor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Provide early, carefully titrated epidural analgesia to blunt pain-induced tachycardia while maintaining preload and systemic vascular resistance
choices.rationale: Correct. In mitral stenosis, tachycardia limits diastolic left ventricular filling across the stenotic valve, so a slowly titrated epidural that prevents pain-induced tachycardia while avoiding abrupt drops in preload and afterload is ideal.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Withhold neuraxial analgesia and rely on labor pain to keep cardiac output elevated
choices.rationale: Labor pain drives tachycardia and increased oxygen demand, which is poorly tolerated in mitral stenosis because it shortens diastolic filling time; good neuraxial analgesia is specifically recommended to prevent this.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Administer a rapid single-shot spinal to achieve a quick, dense block
choices.rationale: A single-shot spinal causes abrupt sympathectomy with sudden falls in preload and afterload, which is poorly tolerated in fixed stenotic lesions; a slowly titrated epidural is preferred to maintain hemodynamic stability.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Target a deliberately high heart rate with an anticholinergic to maximize cardiac output
choices.rationale: Inducing tachycardia is harmful in mitral stenosis because it reduces the diastolic interval needed to fill the left ventricle through the narrowed valve, worsening pulmonary congestion and lowering cardiac output.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Aggressively reduce systemic vascular resistance with a vasodilator infusion throughout labor
choices.rationale: Stenotic lesions tolerate afterload reduction poorly because the fixed obstruction limits the ability to compensate; abrupt drops in systemic vascular resistance can cause hypotension and reduced coronary perfusion.

questions.explanation: Stenotic valvular lesions are less well tolerated in pregnancy than regurgitant lesions, and mitral stenosis is among the most difficult valvulopathies to manage. The key hemodynamic principles are to avoid tachycardia (which shortens diastole and limits left ventricular filling across the narrowed valve), maintain adequate preload, and preserve systemic vascular resistance, using phenylephrine for hypotension. Excellent labor analgesia is recommended specifically to blunt the tachycardic and hyperdynamic response to pain, and this is best accomplished with a slowly titrated segmental lumbar epidural rather than a single-shot spinal, which causes abrupt sympathectomy. Invasive arterial monitoring is often useful for moderate-to-severe stenotic lesions. By contrast, regurgitant lesions benefit from afterload reduction and avoidance of bradycardia.

source.title: Anesthesia for Labor, Delivery, and Cesarean Section in High-Risk Heart Disease - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason: Source loads, is public (NCBI Bookshelf NBK574578, "Anesthesia for Labor, Delivery, and Cesarean Section in High-Risk Heart Disease"), and directly supports the answer. For severe mitral stenosis in labor, the source explicitly recommends segmental lumbar epidural to prevent pain-induced tachycardia while maintaining euvolemia (preload) and stable SVR — matching correct option A. Distractors are correctly wrong: tachycardia impairs LV filling through the stenotic valve, so relying on labor pain (B) or deliberately raising HR with an anticholinergic (D) is harmful; aggressive vasodilation (E) causes hypotension and reflex tachycardia; single-shot spinal (C) produces an abrupt sympathectomy with rapid SVR/preload drop and is described in the source only as a fallback ("next best option") if epidural fails, not a primary strategy. Medicine is accurate and unambiguous; rationales are sound. Safe to publish to the paid bank.

questions.stem: A 31-year-old breastfeeding mother is recovering from an uncomplicated postpartum procedure and requires analgesia. She intends to continue nursing her healthy term infant. Which of the following analgesic plans is most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Scheduled acetaminophen and ibuprofen as first-line, reserving low-dose morphine or hydrocodone for breakthrough pain if needed
choices.rationale: Correct. Acetaminophen and ibuprofen are preferred during lactation because of their low transfer into breast milk and established infant safety; if an opioid is required, morphine or hydrocodone at the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration is preferred.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Scheduled codeine as the primary analgesic
choices.rationale: Codeine is a CYP2D6 prodrug; ultra-rapid metabolizers can convert it to high morphine levels, causing infant sedation and respiratory depression, so codeine should be avoided in breastfeeding mothers.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Scheduled tramadol as the primary analgesic
choices.rationale: Tramadol, like codeine, is metabolized by CYP2D6 to an active metabolite and carries the same risk of excessive infant sedation in ultra-rapid metabolizers; it should be avoided during breastfeeding.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Advise the mother to stop breastfeeding entirely for one week to avoid any drug exposure
choices.rationale: Routine cessation of breastfeeding is unnecessary and harmful to the breastfeeding relationship; safe analgesics exist, and inadequately treated pain itself interferes with successful breastfeeding.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Withhold all analgesics and recommend non-pharmacologic measures only
choices.rationale: Untreated pain interferes with successful breastfeeding and recovery; effective analgesics that are safe in lactation should be provided rather than withholding all pharmacologic pain control.

questions.explanation: For the breastfeeding mother, ibuprofen and acetaminophen are the preferred analgesics because of their low concentration in breast milk and well-established safety even when given directly to infants. When opioids are necessary, the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration is used; morphine and hydrocodone are generally preferred. Codeine and tramadol should be avoided because both are prodrugs metabolized by CYP2D6, and ultra-rapid metabolizers can generate high active-metabolite levels in milk, leading to infant sedation and respiratory depression (the basis for FDA warnings). Importantly, breastfeeding should not be routinely interrupted, since untreated pain itself impairs successful breastfeeding and most analgesics and anesthetic agents transfer in clinically insignificant amounts once the mother is awake and alert.

source.title: Medication Safety in Breastfeeding - American Family Physician (AAFP)

verdict.reason: Verified against the cited AAFP source (https://www.aafp.org/pubs/afp/issues/2022/1200/medication-safety-breastfeeding.html), which loaded as a public page. It directly supports correct answer A: "Ibuprofen and acetaminophen are the preferred agents for pain control during lactation," and "If an oral opioid is required for pain control, hydrocodone or morphine is preferred." The distractors are correctly wrong per the same source: the FDA recommends AVOIDING codeine (B) and tramadol (C) in breastfeeding due to ultra-rapid metabolizer risk of infant sedation/respiratory depression. Stopping breastfeeding for a week (D) is unnecessary given safe analgesic options exist, and withholding all analgesia (E) is inappropriate when the patient requires pain control. Medicine is correct, unambiguous, and rationales are accurate.

questions.stem: A 14-kg, 3-year-old boy presents for open inguinal hernia repair under general anesthesia. After induction and placement of a laryngeal mask airway, you plan a single-shot caudal epidural block with 0.2% ropivacaine to provide intraoperative and postoperative analgesia. You want the sensory block to reach a lower thoracic/upper lumbar dermatomal level adequate to cover the inguinal incision and peritoneal traction. Based on weight-based volume dosing, which volume of local anesthetic is most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.rationale: About 0.25 mL/kg would only reliably cover low sacral dermatomes (e.g., circumcision), well below the thoracolumbar level needed for an inguinal incision with peritoneal traction, risking an inadequate block.

choices.label: B
choices.text: 7 mL (0.5 mL/kg)
choices.rationale: 0.5 mL/kg reliably covers only the sacral dermatomes. It is insufficient to reach the lower thoracic/upper lumbar levels required for inguinal hernia surgery.

choices.label: C
choices.text: 14 mL (1 mL/kg)
choices.rationale: Correct. Approximately 1 mL/kg of local anesthetic achieves lumbar and lower thoracic coverage, the level required for an inguinal hernia incision with peritoneal traction, while keeping the dose within the safe maximum for ropivacaine.

choices.label: D
choices.rationale: 1.25 mL/kg targets mid-thoracic coverage, which exceeds the level needed here and unnecessarily increases the spread and the total local anesthetic dose toward the toxic range.

choices.label: E
choices.text: 28 mL (2 mL/kg)
choices.rationale: This volume far exceeds standard caudal dosing and approaches or exceeds the maximum safe dose of ropivacaine (about 3 mg/kg), creating a substantial risk of local anesthetic systemic toxicity without added benefit.

questions.explanation: Caudal block spread is governed primarily by injected volume (correlating with weight) rather than concentration. A widely taught weight-based scheme is roughly 0.5 mL/kg for sacral coverage, 1 mL/kg for lumbar/lower-thoracic coverage, and 1.25 mL/kg for mid-thoracic coverage. Inguinal hernia repair requires a lower thoracic/upper lumbar level to cover the incision and peritoneal/cord traction, so about 1 mL/kg (14 mL in a 14-kg child) is appropriate. Dilute solutions (e.g., ropivacaine 0.2%, bupivacaine/levobupivacaine 0.25%) are used so that larger covering volumes stay within maximum safe doses (about 3 mg/kg). At 0.2%, 14 mL = 28 mg = 2 mg/kg, which is within limits.

source.title: Pediatric Regional Anesthesia - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason: URL https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK572106/ loads and is public (NCBI Bookshelf StatPearls chapter "Pediatric Regional Anesthesia"). It directly supports the keyed answer, stating: "0.5 mL/kg of LA volume is required for sacral coverage, 1 mL/kg for lumbar and lower thoracic coverage, and 1.25 mL/kg for mid-thoracic coverage" (the Armitage caudal dosing regimen). Medicine is correct and unambiguous: an inguinal hernia repair requires a lower thoracic/upper lumbar sensory level, so 1 mL/kg is appropriate; for a 14-kg child this is 14 mL = option C. Distractors are accurate: A/B (0.5 mL/kg) give only sacral coverage and under-block; D (1.25 mL/kg) targets mid-thoracic (higher than needed); E (2 mL/kg = 28 mL of 0.2% ropivacaine = 56 mg ≈ 4 mg/kg) exceeds the usual ~3 mg/kg ropivacaine ceiling and is excessive. The weight-based volume logic is correctly keyed to C with no factual errors.

questions.stem: A 3-kg infant born at 30 weeks gestation, now 48 weeks postconceptional age, is scheduled for elective inguinal herniorrhaphy at an outpatient surgery center. The infant has had no apneic episodes at home and is otherwise well. The parents ask whether the baby can go home the same day after surgery. Which of the following is the most appropriate management plan?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Proceed as a same-day discharge case because the infant is asymptomatic and feeding well
choices.rationale: Absence of apnea at home does not eliminate postanesthetic apnea risk. A former preterm infant well under the threshold age should not be discharged the same day regardless of being asymptomatic preoperatively.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Admit for at least 12 hours of continuous postoperative apnea/cardiorespiratory monitoring
choices.rationale: Correct. This former preterm infant at 48 weeks postconceptional age is below the commonly cited 60-week threshold and is at risk for postoperative apnea, which may be delayed; guidelines recommend admission with extended (roughly 12 or more hours) apnea monitoring.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Perform a spinal anesthetic so that the infant can be safely discharged home the same day
choices.rationale: While regional (spinal/caudal) anesthesia may reduce apnea incidence, it does not reliably eliminate the risk, and prolonging blocks with adjuncts can itself contribute to apnea. Postoperative monitoring is still required, so same-day discharge is not appropriate.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Discharge home with a parent-operated home apnea monitor and follow-up call the next day
choices.rationale: Home monitoring is not an accepted substitute for in-hospital cardiorespiratory monitoring in a high-risk former preterm infant; postoperative apnea requires immediate intervention that home arrangements cannot reliably provide.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Cancel surgery until the infant reaches 60 weeks postconceptional age
choices.rationale: Cancellation is unnecessary; herniorrhaphy can proceed with appropriate postoperative admission and monitoring. Routinely deferring all such cases is not standard, and incarceration risk may make delay unwise.

questions.explanation: Former preterm infants are at risk for postoperative apnea, which can be delayed for hours after anesthesia. Risk correlates inversely with both gestational age and postconceptional age (PCA); a frequently cited cutoff is PCA under 60 weeks (and gestational age under 37 weeks), below which infants warrant inpatient admission with continuous apnea/cardiorespiratory monitoring (often 12 hours or more, and longer if apnea occurs). Regional techniques may lower but do not abolish the risk, so monitoring is still required. At 48 weeks PCA with prematurity, this infant should be admitted rather than treated as an outpatient.

source.title: Apneas in Infants with Postconceptional Age below 60 Weeks Undergoing Herniorrhaphy - PMC

verdict.reason:
URL loads and is public (PMC4268838, open-access article on postoperative apnea in ex-preterm infants). Medicine is correct and unambiguous: a former 30-week-gestation infant now at 48 weeks postconceptional age undergoing inguinal herniorrhaphy is the classic at-risk scenario for postoperative apnea (well within the <60 weeks PCA risk window, even below the conservative 50-week cutoff). Standard anesthesiology teaching and this source support admission with continuous apnea/cardiorespiratory monitoring rather than same-day discharge, so B is clearly the single best answer.

Distractors are all valid (plausible but wrong): A (same-day discharge) is the trap the scenario is built to refute; C correctly tests the common misconception that spinal/regional anesthesia eliminates apnea risk and permits discharge — it does not, monitoring is still required; D (home apnea monitor) is not an accepted substitute for inpatient postoperative monitoring; E (cancel until 60 weeks) is incorrect/over-conservative since elective herniorrhaphy is managed with admission and monitoring, not categorical deferral.

Rationale/answer accuracy: confirmed correct. One minor note (not disqualifying): the cited article's own dataset states a 'minimum of 10 hours' of monitoring, while option B says 'at least 12 hours.' The 12-hour figure is the broadly accepted consensus standard (and the article itself cites the 12-hour guideline for <46 weeks PCA infants), so 'at least 12 hours' is defensible and does not make B wrong — the threshold and management direction are unambiguous. Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4268838/

questions.stem: A 4-year-old, incompletely immunized child is brought to the emergency department with a 6-hour history of high fever, severe sore throat, muffled 'hot potato' voice, and drooling. The child is anxious, sitting upright leaning forward on both hands, and refuses to lie down. There is soft inspiratory stridor but no barking cough. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Examine the oropharynx with a tongue depressor and obtain a throat swab
choices.rationale: Instrumenting the pharynx or otherwise agitating a child with suspected epiglottitis can precipitate complete airway obstruction and is specifically warned against.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Administer nebulized racemic epinephrine and dexamethasone for croup
choices.rationale: The presentation (high fever, drooling, dysphagia, tripod position, no barking cough) indicates epiglottitis rather than viral croup; racemic epinephrine/steroids treat croup and do not address the immediate airway threat of epiglottitis.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Keep the child calm with a parent and arrange controlled airway management with the most experienced provider in the operating room
choices.rationale: Correct. Suspected epiglottitis is an airway emergency; the child should be kept calm and undisturbed, and the airway secured in a controlled setting (ideally the OR) by the most skilled clinician, with surgical airway backup available.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Obtain a CT scan of the neck to confirm the diagnosis before any intervention
choices.rationale: Transporting an unstable child for CT and forcing positioning can provoke obstruction; definitive management is securing the airway in a controlled setting, not radiographic confirmation.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Place the child supine and start high-flow nasal oxygen
choices.rationale: Forcing a child with epiglottitis to lie supine can worsen obstruction; these patients protect their own airway in the upright tripod position and should not be made to lie flat.

questions.explanation: Epiglottitis classically presents with the rapid onset of high fever and the 'three Ds' (drooling, dysphagia, distress), muffled voice, tripod/sniffing positioning, and minimal cough, most often in incompletely immunized children (historically Haemophilus influenzae type b). This contrasts with viral croup (laryngotracheobronchitis), which features a barking/seal-like cough, hoarseness, low-grade fever, and a gradual course, and which lacks prominent drooling and dysphagia. The cardinal management principle for suspected epiglottitis is to avoid agitating the child (no tongue blade, no forced supine positioning, no painful procedures) and to secure the airway in a controlled environment such as the operating room with the most experienced personnel and surgical airway backup.

questions.stem: A term neonate with prenatally diagnosed left-sided congenital diaphragmatic hernia (CDH) is intubated in the delivery room and brought to the OR for repair. The infant has a scaphoid abdomen and decreased breath sounds on the left. Which of the following intraoperative management strategies is most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Provide vigorous bag-mask ventilation before intubation to optimize preoxygenation
choices.rationale: Mask ventilation should be avoided in CDH because it insufflates the stomach and bowel within the chest, further compressing the hypoplastic lung and worsening ventilation and venous return.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Use gentle ventilation with peak inspiratory pressure under 25 cmH2O, accept permissive hypercapnia, and target preductal SpO2 of 85-95%
choices.rationale: Correct. Lung-protective 'gentle' ventilation limiting PIP (under about 25 cmH2O) with permissive hypercapnia and preductal SpO2 targets of 85-95% minimizes barotrauma to the hypoplastic lungs and is associated with improved survival.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hyperventilate to a PaCO2 of 25-30 mmHg to induce alkalosis and reduce pulmonary vascular resistance
choices.rationale: Aggressive hyperventilation/forced alkalosis is an outdated strategy that increases ventilator-induced lung injury and worsens outcomes; modern care uses permissive hypercapnia instead.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Leave the stomach decompressed only by avoiding feeds; an orogastric tube is contraindicated
choices.rationale: The opposite is true: a naso- or orogastric tube to continuous low suction is recommended to decompress the herniated stomach/bowel and reduce intrathoracic compression.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Maintain high PEEP of 8-10 cmH2O to recruit the ipsilateral hypoplastic lung
choices.rationale: High PEEP risks overdistending the contralateral lung and barotrauma; a modest PEEP of about 3-5 cmH2O is used as part of a gentle ventilation strategy.

questions.explanation: CDH is associated with pulmonary hypoplasia and pulmonary hypertension. The cornerstone of perioperative management is 'gentle ventilation': limiting peak inspiratory pressure (roughly under 25 cmH2O), using modest PEEP (3-5 cmH2O), tolerating permissive hypercapnia (PaCO2 about 45-60 mmHg), and targeting preductal SpO2 of 85-95% to avoid ventilator-induced lung injury. Older strategies of hyperventilation to induce alkalosis increased mortality and have been abandoned. Mask ventilation is avoided because gastric/bowel insufflation worsens lung compression; a naso/orogastric tube to low continuous suction is placed for decompression. High-frequency oscillatory ventilation is reserved for when conventional ventilation fails.

Question stem (left CDH, scaphoid abdomen, decreased left breath sounds) is clinically accurate. All rationales accurate. Safe to publish.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old, 7-kg infant undergoes a 90-minute laparoscopic procedure. You are selecting an intraoperative maintenance fluid. The infant is healthy and was not preterm. Which fluid choice best reflects current pediatric perioperative fluid recommendations to minimize the risks of both hyponatremia and hypoglycemia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 0.45% saline with 5% dextrose (a hypotonic, high-glucose solution)
choices.rationale: Hypotonic fluids markedly increase the risk of hospital-acquired hyponatremia (and cerebral edema), and 5% dextrose can cause hyperglycemia; this combination is no longer recommended for perioperative maintenance.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A balanced isotonic crystalloid with 1-2.5% dextrose
choices.rationale: Correct. A balanced isotonic solution minimizes the risk of hyponatremia, while a low dextrose concentration (1-2.5%) guards against hypoglycemia in a young child without provoking the hyperglycemia seen with 5% dextrose.

choices.label: C
choices.text: 0.9% saline with 5% dextrose
choices.rationale: Although isotonic in sodium, the 5% dextrose concentration is excessive for routine intraoperative maintenance and risks hyperglycemia; lower glucose concentrations (1-2.5%) are preferred in young children.

choices.label: D
choices.text: 5% dextrose in water (D5W) alone
choices.rationale: D5W is effectively electrolyte-free and behaves as free water once glucose is metabolized, causing severe hyponatremia; it is dangerous as a maintenance fluid.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A balanced isotonic crystalloid with no added dextrose
choices.rationale: An isotonic dextrose-free fluid avoids hyponatremia, but a 6-month-old has limited glycogen reserves and is at risk for intraoperative hypoglycemia, so adding low-concentration dextrose is preferable for this age group.

questions.explanation: Modern pediatric perioperative fluid practice favors balanced isotonic crystalloids for maintenance because hypotonic fluids substantially raise the risk of hospital-acquired hyponatremia, which can cause cerebral edema and seizures (children have a larger brain-to-skull ratio). At the same time, routine 5% dextrose is no longer advised for healthy children because of hyperglycemia risk. For infants and children under 2 years (and those at risk of hypoglycemia from limited glycogen stores), adding a low glucose concentration of 1-2.5% balances the competing risks of hypo- and hyperglycemia. Rate is still estimated by the Holliday-Segar 4-2-1 rule, but the composition has shifted to isotonic, low-glucose solutions.

source.title: Pediatric perioperative fluid management - PMC

verdict.reason: Source (https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8477784/) loads, is public, and directly supports the answer. It states current guidelines recommend "balanced solutions with 1-2.5% Dextrose" and "isotonic fluids with lower glucose concentration (1-2.5%) for children less than 2 years" — exactly option B. Medicine is correct and unambiguous: B (balanced isotonic crystalloid with 1-2.5% dextrose) is the best choice for a 6-month-old (<2 years) to balance hyponatremia risk (addressed by isotonic/balanced fluid) and hypoglycemia risk (addressed by low-dose 1-2.5% dextrose). Distractors are correctly inferior: A (0.45% saline+5% dextrose) and D (D5W) are hypotonic and cause hyponatremia; C (0.9% saline+5% dextrose) carries hyperglycemia risk and is not a balanced solution; E (balanced isotonic, no dextrose) is reasonable but, per the source, infants <2 years specifically warrant 1-2.5% dextrose, making B superior to E for this patient. Patient framing (healthy, non-preterm) appropriately excludes both routine high-dose dextrose and the special hypoglycemia-risk exceptions. Rationale is accurate and clinically sound for a paid bank.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old child undergoes bilateral myringotomy tube placement with sevoflurane via mask, with no IV access until the end of the case. In the PACU the child awakens thrashing, inconsolable, not making eye contact, and not recognizing the parent, with no apparent pain from the brief procedure. The team wants to reduce the likelihood of this complication in future similar cases. Which intervention is best supported for prophylaxis of emergence agitation after sevoflurane?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administer dexmedetomidine during the case
choices.rationale: Correct. Dexmedetomidine, an alpha-2 adrenergic agonist with sedative and analgesic properties, reduces the incidence and severity of sevoflurane-associated emergence agitation when given prophylactically.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Substitute desflurane maintenance to deepen and speed emergence
choices.rationale: Desflurane is also associated with emergence agitation and is pungent/airway-irritating; switching to it is not an established strategy to prevent agitation and is unsuitable for inhalational maintenance via mask in young children.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Give a higher dose of an anticholinergic such as atropine at induction
choices.rationale: Anticholinergics are not used to prevent emergence agitation; central anticholinergic effects could worsen, not reduce, postoperative agitation/delirium.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Plan for more rapid emergence by lightening anesthesia near the end of surgery
choices.rationale: Abrupt or rapid awakening does not prevent emergence agitation and may increase it; the rapid offset of sevoflurane is part of why agitation is common, so simply speeding emergence is not protective.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Withhold all analgesia to avoid oversedation in the PACU
choices.rationale: Untreated pain can mimic or trigger agitation; withholding analgesia tends to increase, not decrease, the likelihood of emergence agitation.

questions.explanation: Emergence agitation (emergence delirium) is common after sevoflurane, especially in preschool-aged children, and typically occurs within the first 30 minutes of recovery with nonpurposeful thrashing, inconsolability, and lack of recognition of caregivers. Effective prophylactic strategies include intraoperative dexmedetomidine (an alpha-2 agonist), as well as adequate analgesia and propofol; opioids such as fentanyl also reduce incidence but in head-to-head data dexmedetomidine performs at least as well in the early emergence period. Simply changing to desflurane, speeding emergence, giving anticholinergics, or withholding analgesia are not supported and may worsen agitation.

source.title: Fentanyl Versus Dexmedetomidine for the Prevention of Emergence Agitation in Children After Sevoflurane Anaesthesia - PMC

URL loads and is public (PMC9521508, open-access NCBI/PMC article). It directly supports the answer: the study concludes prophylactic dexmedetomidine effectively prevents emergence agitation after sevoflurane in children (PAED scores significantly lower vs comparator), and it confirms sevoflurane is associated with higher EA incidence — supporting option A.

Medicine is correct and unambiguous. The vignette (4-yr-old, bilateral myringotomy tubes, sevoflurane via mask, no IV, thrashing/inconsolable/no eye contact/non-recognition with no pain source) is a classic emergence delirium presentation, and dexmedetomidine is the best-supported pharmacologic prophylaxis.

Distractors are all correctly wrong: (B) desflurane is itself associated with high EA rates and substituting it to "deepen/speed emergence" is flawed reasoning, not evidence-based prevention; (C) anticholinergics like atropine do not prevent EA and central anticholinergic effects can worsen delirium; (D) rapid emergence/lightening anesthesia is a recognized contributor to EA, so this worsens risk; (E) untreated pain is a major EA contributor, so withholding analgesia worsens it. Single best answer is A and rationales are accurate.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with severe pulmonary arterial hypertension is undergoing mitral valve repair. After separation from cardiopulmonary bypass, his pulmonary artery pressures rise, the right ventricle distends and becomes hypokinetic, and systemic pressure falls. The team wants to lower pulmonary vascular resistance while avoiding worsening of his already low systemic blood pressure. Which therapy best achieves selective pulmonary vasodilation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inhaled nitric oxide
choices.rationale: Correct. Inhaled nitric oxide reaches only ventilated lung units and is rapidly inactivated by binding to hemoglobin once it reaches the bloodstream, so it lowers pulmonary artery pressure and pulmonary vascular resistance with little systemic hypotension.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intravenous sodium nitroprusside
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Nitroprusside is a nonselective systemic and pulmonary vasodilator and would worsen this patient's systemic hypotension.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intravenous phenylephrine
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Phenylephrine is a vasoconstrictor that raises systemic pressure but tends to increase, not decrease, pulmonary vascular resistance; it does not provide selective pulmonary vasodilation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intravenous nitroglycerin infusion
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Intravenous nitroglycerin acts on the systemic venous and arterial beds and lowers systemic pressure; it is not a selective pulmonary vasodilator.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Intravenous esmolol
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Esmolol is a beta-blocker that reduces contractility and heart rate; it does not selectively dilate the pulmonary vasculature and could further compromise the failing right ventricle.

questions.explanation: Inhaled nitric oxide is the prototypical selective pulmonary vasodilator. Because it is delivered to ventilated alveoli and is immediately scavenged by hemoglobin on reaching the blood, its vasodilating effect is confined to the pulmonary vasculature - decreasing pulmonary artery pressure and pulmonary vascular resistance without meaningful systemic hypotension. This is ideal in acute right ventricular failure from pulmonary hypertension after bypass. Note that abrupt discontinuation can cause rebound pulmonary hypertension, so weaning must be gradual.

source.title: Nitric Oxide - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

It supports the answer: it explicitly states iNO "causes pulmonary vasodilation with little chance of hypotension or systemic vasodilation" because NO is rapidly inactivated by hemoglobin once in the bloodstream — the exact selectivity mechanism the question tests. Medicine is correct and unambiguous: A (inhaled nitric oxide) is the only selective pulmonary vasodilator. Distractors are clearly inferior — nitroprusside (B) and nitroglycerin (D) are nonselective systemic vasodilators that would worsen the existing hypotension; phenylephrine (C) is a vasoconstrictor that does not lower (and may raise) PVR; esmolol (E) is a beta-blocker that would worsen RV failure and hypotension. The scenario (post-CPB acute RV failure from pulmonary hypertension with systemic hypotension) is a classic, valid indication. Rationales are accurate.

questions.stem: A 46-year-old man with a malignant pericardial effusion and clinical cardiac tamponade (BP 92/74 mmHg, HR 118/min, pulsus paradoxus) requires general anesthesia because he cannot tolerate the procedure awake. The surgeon is scrubbed and ready to drain the pericardium immediately. Which induction approach best matches the hemodynamic goals for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Maintain preload, heart rate, contractility, and afterload using a ketamine-based induction with the surgical team prepped to drain immediately
choices.rationale: Correct. Tamponade physiology demands 'fast, full, and tight' - preserved preload, heart rate, contractility, and systemic vascular resistance. Ketamine preserves sympathetic tone, and having the surgeon ready to decompress at induction limits the risk of collapse.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Deepen with a high-dose propofol bolus to blunt the sympathetic response before laryngoscopy
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Propofol causes venodilation, myocardial depression, and loss of sympathetic tone - exactly the changes that precipitate cardiovascular collapse in a preload-dependent, sympathetically driven patient.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Administer a generous dose of an opioid plus a beta-blocker to control the tachycardia before induction
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The tachycardia is a compensatory mechanism maintaining cardiac output; blunting heart rate and sympathetic drive removes the patient's primary compensation and risks decompensation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Induce with a volatile agent by mask to preserve spontaneous ventilation while keeping the patient deeply anesthetized
choices.rationale: Incorrect. While maintaining spontaneous ventilation is desirable, deep volatile anesthesia causes dose-dependent myocardial depression and vasodilation, undermining the contractility and afterload these patients depend on.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Aggressively diurese to reduce ventricular wall stress before induction
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Tamponade patients are preload-dependent; diuresis reduces venous return and would worsen hemodynamics, the opposite of the 'full' goal.

questions.explanation: Cardiac tamponade impairs diastolic filling, so cardiac output depends on maintaining preload (full), heart rate and contractility (fast), and systemic vascular resistance (tight). Patients rely on heightened sympathetic tone and compensatory tachycardia. Induction of general anesthesia is the most dangerous moment because loss of sympathetic tone, venodilation, myocardial depression, and positive-pressure ventilation all reduce venous return and can cause collapse. Ketamine is favored because it preserves sympathetic tone and spontaneous ventilation, and ideally the surgeon is prepped to drain the effusion the moment anesthesia is induced.

source.title: Cardiac Tamponade - OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason:
URL loads and is public (openanesthesia.org keyword page on cardiac tamponade). It directly supports correct answer A. The page states hemodynamic goals as "Fast, Full, and Tight" — maintain heart rate/contractility, preload, and SVR/afterload — matching option A's "maintain preload, heart rate, contractility, and afterload." It states "Ketamine may be considered to preserve sympathetic tone and spontaneous ventilation," supporting the ketamine-based induction, and notes induction is a high-risk period favoring immediate surgical readiness to drain.

All distractors are correctly wrong and supported by the source: B (high-dose propofol) is explicitly listed under "Avoid... high-dose propofol" (myocardial depression/venodilation, drops preload and contractility). C (opioid + beta-blocker) is dangerous because the tachycardia is a compensatory mechanism that must be preserved; beta-blockade removes compensation and generous opioid blunts sympathetic tone. D (deep volatile) is explicitly under "Avoid... volatile anesthetics" — although it preserves spontaneous ventilation, deep volatile causes myocardial depression and vasodilation, making it net harmful. E (aggressive diuresis) directly opposes the "Full"/maintain-preload goal and worsens tamponade.

Medicine is correct, single best answer, unambiguous. Rationales accurate. Safe to publish.

questions.stem: An anesthesiologist plans intraoperative transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) for a patient undergoing mitral valve surgery. Which of the following patient findings is an absolute contraindication to placing the TEE probe?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A known esophageal stricture
choices.rationale: Correct. Esophageal disease with a known stricture is an absolute contraindication because passing the probe risks esophageal perforation, the most feared complication of TEE.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A small asymptomatic hiatal hernia
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Hiatal hernia is generally a relative contraindication, not an absolute one; TEE can often proceed with caution.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mild cervical spine arthritis
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Cervical spine disease is a relative contraindication requiring care during probe placement, not an absolute bar to TEE.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A platelet count of 90,000/microliter
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Coagulopathy is considered a relative contraindication; mild thrombocytopenia does not absolutely preclude TEE.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Prior chest radiation for breast cancer
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Prior chest radiation is a relative contraindication, weighed against the benefit of the study rather than an absolute prohibition.

questions.explanation: Absolute contraindications to TEE involve esophageal or gastric pathology that makes probe passage hazardous: known esophageal stricture, diverticula, varices or tumor, prior esophageal or gastric surgery, perforated viscus, and an uncooperative patient. Esophageal perforation, though rare (about 0.03%), is the most serious complication, which is why structural esophageal disease such as stricture is an absolute bar. Findings such as hiatal hernia, cervical spine disease, coagulopathy, and prior chest radiation are relative contraindications, where TEE may proceed if benefit outweighs risk.

source.title: Transesophageal Echocardiogram - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

It explicitly lists "esophageal disease with known stricture" among ABSOLUTE contraindications to TEE probe placement, confirming option A. It explicitly lists the four distractors as RELATIVE contraindications: "cervical spine disease, hiatal hernia, coagulopathy, prior chest radiation" — covering B (hiatal hernia), C (cervical spine arthritis), D (coagulopathy/thrombocytopenia; a platelet count of 90,000 is only mild and at most relative), and E (prior chest radiation for breast cancer). Medicine is correct and unambiguous: among the options only A is an absolute contraindication. Rationales are accurate. Safe to publish.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman is undergoing an awake craniotomy with cortical language mapping for a left frontal low-grade glioma. The scalp block has been placed and she is comfortable and conversant. As the neurosurgeon begins direct electrical cortical stimulation, the patient's right arm and face begin to twitch rhythmically and she stops responding to commands. Vital signs are stable and the airway is patent. What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Irrigate the exposed cortex with ice-cold sterile saline
choices.rationale: Stimulation-induced focal seizures are a common event during cortical mapping. The recognized first-line maneuver is irrigation of the cortical surface with ice-cold (iced) sterile saline, which rapidly terminates most stimulation-evoked seizures while preserving the patient's ability to participate in mapping.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Administer a 100 mg bolus of intravenous propofol and prepare to convert to general anesthesia
choices.rationale: A large sedating/anesthetic dose is excessive for a self-limited stimulation-evoked seizure and would abolish the awake state needed for mapping. If seizures persist after cold irrigation, small titrated doses of propofol (10-50 mg) or midazolam (1-2 mg) are used — not an immediate 100 mg bolus with conversion to general anesthesia.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Load with intravenous phenytoin 20 mg/kg
choices.rationale: A full antiepileptic loading dose treats sustained or generalized seizure activity, not a brief focal seizure provoked by the stimulating electrode. It does not address the immediate stimulus and would not be the first maneuver.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Immediately repeat the same-intensity cortical stimulation to confirm the eloquent area
choices.rationale: Re-stimulating the same site at the same current during an active seizure risks propagation to a generalized seizure. Stimulation should be paused and the seizure terminated before any further mapping.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Hyperventilate the patient to an end-tidal CO2 of 25 mmHg
choices.rationale: Hyperventilation is a brain-relaxation maneuver under general anesthesia and is impractical in a spontaneously breathing awake patient; it does not abort a cortical stimulation seizure and risks cerebral vasoconstriction.

questions.explanation: During awake craniotomy, direct cortical stimulation for functional mapping can provoke focal (and occasionally generalized) seizures in roughly 2-22% of cases. The first-line and most effective immediate treatment is irrigation of the exposed cortex with ice-cold sterile saline, which terminates most stimulation-evoked seizures within seconds and allows mapping to continue. Only if the seizure persists are small titrated boluses of IV propofol (10-50 mg) or midazolam (1-2 mg) given. Recognizing the cold-saline maneuver as the reflexive first step is a high-yield point for the anesthetist managing these cases.

source.title: Anesthesia for Awake Craniotomy — StatPearls (NCBI Bookshelf)

verdict.reason: URL loads, public (NCBI/StatPearls "Anesthesia for Awake Craniotomy"), and directly supports the answer. The source states under Complications: "First-line management involves irrigation of the cortex with ice-cold sterile saline, repeated as needed," confirming option A. Medicine is correct and unambiguous: stimulation-induced intraoperative seizures during awake craniotomy are typically focal/brief/self-limiting and treated first-line with cold saline irrigation. Distractors are correctly wrong: (B) propofol/GA is a backup only if irrigation fails, and the cited dose (10–50 mg) is lower than the 100 mg bolus listed; (C) phenytoin loading is too slow and not indicated for brief self-limiting events; (D) repeating same-intensity stimulation would re-trigger/prolong the seizure; (E) hyperventilation to ETCO2 25 is not standard management and lowers seizure threshold. Single best answer is clearly A; rationale and source are accurate.

questions.stem: A 54-year-old woman is admitted after aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage and undergoes successful endovascular coiling on day 0. On postoperative day 6 she develops new confusion and left-sided weakness; transcranial Doppler shows elevated velocities consistent with cerebral vasospasm. Which intervention has been shown to improve long-term neurologic outcome and should be administered routinely from the time of diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Oral nimodipine 60 mg every 4 hours for 21 days
choices.rationale: Oral nimodipine is the only therapy proven to improve long-term functional/neurologic outcomes after aneurysmal SAH. Guidelines recommend 60 mg every 4 hours for 21 days started early after the hemorrhage. Its benefit is thought to be neuroprotective rather than from reliably reversing angiographic vasospasm.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prophylactic intravenous magnesium infusion to a supratherapeutic level
choices.rationale: Despite mechanistic appeal, randomized trials have not shown that supratherapeutic magnesium improves outcome after SAH, and it is not a standard outcome-improving therapy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Routine prophylactic 'triple-H' therapy (hypervolemia, hemodilution, hypertension)
choices.rationale: Triple-H therapy has not been demonstrated to improve overall outcome; induced hypervolemia and hemodilution may be harmful. Current practice favors euvolemia with induced hypertension if delayed ischemia develops, rather than routine prophylactic triple-H.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intravenous nimodipine titrated to a higher dose than the oral regimen
choices.rationale: The evidence base and approved regimen for outcome benefit is oral nimodipine. IV formulations cause profound hypotension and are not the standard outcome-improving therapy; reducing the nimodipine dose (e.g., for hypotension) is actually associated with worse outcomes.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Prophylactic lumbar drain placement to reduce blood burden
choices.rationale: CSF diversion may help selected patients with hydrocephalus or clot burden but is not the established intervention that improves long-term neurologic outcome and is not given routinely to every SAH patient from diagnosis.

questions.explanation: Among the many therapies tried for aneurysmal SAH and its delayed cerebral ischemia, oral nimodipine (60 mg every 4 hours for 21 days) is the single agent with proven improvement in long-term functional outcome and is recommended for essentially all patients. Notably, nimodipine improves outcome largely through neuroprotection rather than by consistently reversing the angiographic vasospasm itself. Triple-H therapy, once widely used, has not been shown to improve outcome and carries risk from hypervolemia and hemodilution; modern management emphasizes euvolemia with induced hypertension when delayed cerebral ischemia is present. Vasospasm risk peaks roughly days 4-14 post-hemorrhage, consistent with this patient's day-6 presentation.

source.title: Nimodipine — StatPearls (NCBI Bookshelf)

verdict.reason: Source (https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK534870/) is public, loads, and is the StatPearls "Nimodipine" article. It directly supports correct answer A: it cites the Neurocritical Care Society recommendation of oral nimodipine 60 mg every 4 hours for 21 days after SAH, and states nimodipine reduces cerebral infarction and improves neurologic outcomes by reducing ischemic deficits. Medicine is correct and the keyed answer is unambiguous: oral nimodipine is the only listed intervention proven to improve long-term neurologic outcome. Distractors are all accurate and defensible: prophylactic IV magnesium showed no outcome benefit (MASH-2/IMASH); routine prophylactic triple-H is not recommended (euvolemia preferred); IV nimodipine titrated higher is not the standard (oral route is the evidence-based one and IV is not FDA-approved in the US); prophylactic lumbar drain is not an established routine outcome-improving therapy. Rationales accurate. Minor non-disqualifying nuance: nimodipine is in practice begun at SAH diagnosis (day 0) and acts via neuroprotection rather than reversing vasospasm, but the option text and best-answer logic remain correct.

questions.stem: A 46-year-old man undergoes endoscopic transsphenoidal resection of a pituitary macroadenoma. On the first postoperative night the nurse reports urine output of 350-450 mL/h for three consecutive hours. Laboratory and bedside data show serum sodium 149 mEq/L, rising serum osmolality, urine osmolality 110 mOsm/kg, and urine specific gravity 1.003. He is awake and thirsty. Which is the most appropriate next step?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administer desmopressin and ensure access to free water / match urine losses
choices.rationale: The picture — high-volume dilute urine (low urine osmolality and specific gravity) with rising serum sodium and osmolality after pituitary surgery — is central diabetes insipidus from AVP (vasopressin) deficiency. Desmopressin replaces ADH, and free-water replacement corrects the developing hypernatremia.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Restrict free water and start a fluid restriction protocol for SIADH
choices.rationale: SIADH produces concentrated urine with hyponatremia. Here the urine is dilute and the sodium is high, which is the opposite pattern. Fluid restriction would worsen the hypernatremia and hypovolemia of diabetes insipidus.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Give a 1 L bolus of 3% hypertonic saline
choices.rationale: Hypertonic saline would aggravate the already elevated serum sodium. The problem is electrolyte-free water loss, not sodium deficit; this could precipitate dangerous hypernatremia.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Administer intravenous furosemide to reduce the polyuria
choices.rationale: A loop diuretic increases urinary water and solute loss, worsening the volume depletion and hypernatremia of diabetes insipidus. It does not address the underlying ADH deficiency.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Reassure and observe; this degree of polyuria is expected mobilization of operative fluids
choices.rationale: Mobilized perioperative fluid produces an appropriately concentrated or iso-osmolar urine with stable/low-normal sodium. Dilute urine with rising sodium is pathologic and requires treatment, not observation.

questions.explanation: Central (neurogenic) diabetes insipidus is a recognized early complication of transsphenoidal pituitary surgery, caused by transient AVP deficiency after manipulation of the hypothalamic-neurohypophyseal axis. The diagnostic signature is hypotonic polyuria — high urine output (e.g., >4 mL/kg/h or >50 mL/kg/24h) with low urine osmolality and low specific gravity — accompanied by rising serum sodium and osmolality. Management is desmopressin plus replacement of free-water losses (and access to oral water in the awake, thirsty patient). The key distractor is SIADH, which shows the mirror-image laboratory pattern (concentrated urine, hyponatremia) and is managed with fluid restriction; confusing the two leads to dangerous mismanagement.

Per-option rationales are accurate. questions.stem: A 61-year-old man has been in the ICU for 12 days with severe sepsis, intubated and managed for several days with a continuous infusion of a nondepolarizing neuromuscular blocker that was stopped 48 hours ago. He now requires emergent re-intubation. Why is succinylcholine relatively contraindicated in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prolonged immobilization and chemical denervation have upregulated extrajunctional acetylcholine receptors, so succinylcholine can cause exaggerated potassium efflux and life-threatening hyperkalemia
choices.rationale: Critical illness, prolonged immobility, and 'chemical denervation' from sustained nondepolarizing blockade cause proliferation of immature/extrajunctional ACh receptors across the muscle membrane. These receptors stay open longer; succinylcholine depolarizes them and produces a massive potassium efflux, risking hyperkalemic cardiac arrest.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Residual nondepolarizing blocker will antagonize succinylcholine and render it completely ineffective
choices.rationale: Although nondepolarizing agents can blunt the fasciculations and onset, the binding interaction is not the relative contraindication and does not make succinylcholine wholly ineffective. The danger is receptor upregulation causing hyperkalemia, not a competitive failure to work.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sepsis depletes plasma pseudocholinesterase, causing a malignant hyperthermia reaction
choices.rationale: Reduced pseudocholinesterase prolongs the block but does not cause hyperkalemia or malignant hyperthermia. Malignant hyperthermia is an unrelated pharmacogenetic disorder of calcium handling, not a consequence of sepsis.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Succinylcholine in critically ill patients reliably triggers malignant hyperthermia
choices.rationale: Malignant hyperthermia susceptibility is genetic and is not conferred by critical illness or chronic paralysis. The specific risk in this scenario is receptor-mediated hyperkalemia.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Downregulation of acetylcholine receptors makes succinylcholine cause profound prolonged paralysis
choices.rationale: The pathophysiology is upregulation (not downregulation) of receptors, and the feared complication is acute hyperkalemia rather than prolonged paralysis. This reverses the actual mechanism.

questions.explanation: In states of denervation, prolonged immobilization, burns, upper/lower motor neuron injury, and 'chemical denervation' from prolonged nondepolarizing neuromuscular blockade (as in this ICU patient), acetylcholine receptors are upregulated 2- to 100-fold and immature/extrajunctional isoforms proliferate along the entire muscle membrane. These immature receptors remain open 2-10 times longer than mature receptors. When succinylcholine depolarizes this expanded receptor population, a large efflux of potassium occurs, which can precipitate life-threatening hyperkalemia and cardiac arrest. This is why succinylcholine is avoided in chronically paralyzed or critically ill immobilized patients, and a nondepolarizing agent (e.g., rocuronium) is preferred.

source.title: Suxamethonium-Induced Hyperkalemia: A Short Review of Causes and Recommendations for Clinical Applications — PMC

It directly supports answer A: immobilization, denervation, and critical illness upregulate extrajunctional ACh receptors (by 2-100x), causing exaggerated potassium efflux and life-threatening hyperkalemia; lethal hyperkalemic responses to suxamethonium are reported in critically ill patients, and ICU stay >16 days is a noted risk factor. The clinical vignette is a classic setup: 12 days in ICU plus chemical denervation from prolonged nondepolarizing NMB infusion plus immobilization = upregulated extrajunctional receptors and hyperkalemia risk. The "stopped 48 hours ago" detail correctly removes residual blocker as the issue. All distractors are accurately wrong: B (no residual blocker after 48h, and depolarizers aren't "completely" antagonized); C (sepsis/pseudocholinesterase issues cause prolonged paralysis, not MH); D (succinylcholine does not "reliably" trigger MH, which requires genetic susceptibility); E (receptors are upregulated not downregulated, and the hazard is hyperkalemia not prolonged paralysis). Rationale for correct answer matches the source. Safe to publish.

questions.stem: During a complex aortic arch procedure, a patient is monitored with bilateral near-infrared spectroscopy (NIRS) cerebral oximetry. The right-sided regional cerebral oxygen saturation (rSO2) falls from a baseline of 68% to 52% (a sustained decline of >20% from baseline), while the left remains near baseline. Which of the following is the most appropriate FIRST corrective action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Check head and perfusion cannula position to ensure there is no mechanical obstruction to cerebral venous or arterial flow
choices.rationale: A unilateral desaturation strongly suggests a focal mechanical cause — head malposition or a malpositioned/obstructing arterial cannula compromising flow to one hemisphere. Verifying and correcting head and cannula position is a simple, high-yield first step before escalating systemic interventions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Immediately transfuse packed red blood cells regardless of hemoglobin
choices.rationale: Transfusion is one corrective measure when anemia limits oxygen delivery, but it is not the first step for an acute unilateral drop and is typically considered at a hemoglobin around 8-9 g/dL — not reflexively or regardless of hemoglobin.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decrease the depth of anesthesia to raise cerebral metabolic demand
choices.rationale: Reducing anesthetic depth increases cerebral oxygen consumption and would tend to lower, not raise, rSO2. Increasing anesthetic depth (reducing demand) is part of the corrective bundle, the opposite of this option.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Induce hyperventilation to lower PaCO2 toward 25 mmHg
choices.rationale: Hypocapnia causes cerebral vasoconstriction and reduces cerebral blood flow, worsening desaturation. The correct direction is to increase PaCO2 toward normocapnia (~40 mmHg) to promote cerebral vasodilation.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lower the mean arterial pressure to reduce shear on the arch repair
choices.rationale: Raising — not lowering — MAP is a recognized intervention to improve cerebral perfusion when rSO2 falls. Lowering MAP would further compromise cerebral oxygen delivery.

questions.explanation: A sustained fall in rSO2 of more than ~20% from the individual baseline (or an absolute value below ~50%) is the accepted trigger for intervention with NIRS cerebral oximetry. A structured corrective bundle includes checking head and cannula position, raising MAP (e.g., with vasopressors), increasing FiO2, restoring normocapnia by increasing PaCO2 toward ~40 mmHg, increasing anesthetic depth to reduce cerebral metabolic demand, optimizing cardiac output, and transfusing for significant anemia. A unilateral desaturation in particular points to a focal/mechanical cause, so confirming head and perfusion-cannula position is the logical first maneuver before escalating systemic measures.

source.title: Association between Cerebral Oxygen Saturation with Outcome in Cardiac Surgery: Brain as an Index Organ — PMC

The stem's "sustained decline of >20% from baseline" matches the article's defined significant-desaturation threshold ("a 20% reduction from baseline values"). A unilateral drop (right falls, left near baseline) is the classic clue for a mechanical/positional cause (e.g., head malposition or cannula obstructing cerebral arterial inflow/venous outflow on one side), so checking head/cannula position as the immediate, benign, reversible first action is correct and unambiguous.

Distractors all clearly wrong and consistent with the source:
