The intermittent/irregular character of the spikes correctly distinguishes cannon a waves of AV dissociation. Medicine is correct, unambiguous, and rationales are accurate.

questions.stem: You are setting up monitors before inducing general anesthesia. Which of the following is explicitly required by the ASA Standards for Basic Anesthetic Monitoring?

choices.label: A
choices.text: When pulse oximetry is used, the variable-pitch pulse tone and the low-threshold alarm must be audible
choices.rationale: Correct. The ASA standard specifies that to assess oxygenation, pulse oximetry shall be employed with the variable pitch pulse tone and the low threshold alarm audible to the anesthesia team. This audible feedback is an explicit requirement of the standard.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A processed EEG (e.g., BIS) monitor must be applied for every general anesthetic
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Processed EEG/depth-of-anesthesia monitoring is not a required component of the ASA basic monitoring standard; it is optional and not addressed as mandatory.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Arterial blood pressure must be determined at least every 15 minutes
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The standard requires arterial blood pressure and heart rate to be determined at least every 5 minutes, not every 15.

choices.label: D
choices.text: An intra-arterial catheter is required for all general anesthetics
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Invasive arterial monitoring is not mandated; non-invasive blood pressure measurement satisfies the circulation requirement for routine cases.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Core temperature must be continuously measured in every patient regardless of circumstances
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Temperature must be monitored when clinically significant changes in body temperature are intended, anticipated, or suspected, not unconditionally in every case.

questions.explanation: The ASA Standards for Basic Anesthetic Monitoring require continual evaluation of oxygenation, ventilation, circulation, and temperature. For oxygenation, pulse oximetry is used with the variable pitch pulse tone and low threshold alarm audible. Blood pressure and heart rate are determined at least every 5 minutes; ECG is monitored continuously; temperature is monitored when significant change is intended, anticipated, or suspected. Processed EEG and invasive arterial lines are not required by the basic standard.

source.title: ASA Standards for Basic Anesthetic Monitoring - OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason: URL loads and is public (openanesthesia.org keyword page). Source supports correct answer A: ASA Standards for Basic Anesthetic Monitoring require pulse oximetry "with audible tone and alarm," i.e., the variable-pitch pulse tone and low-threshold alarm must be audible. All distractors are correctly false per the source: (B) no processed EEG/BIS mandate for every GA; (C) BP must be measured at least every 5 minutes, not 15 — well-chosen distractor; (D) intra-arterial monitoring is one optional method, not required for all; (E) temperature monitoring required only when clinically significant temperature changes are intended/expected/suspected, not continuously in every patient. Medicine is correct and unambiguous; the single correct answer holds. Safe to publish.

questions.subtopic: Ventilator modes / pressure vs volume control
questions.stem: An intubated patient is ventilated in pressure-control mode with inspiratory pressure 18 cm H2O above PEEP, delivering a tidal volume of about 520 mL. The surgeon then establishes pneumoperitoneum and places the patient in steep Trendelenburg, abruptly reducing thoracopulmonary compliance. With no change to the ventilator settings, what will most likely happen?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Delivered tidal volume will fall, risking hypoventilation
choices.rationale: Correct. In pressure-control ventilation the clinician fixes the inspiratory pressure, so tidal volume is the dependent variable. When compliance decreases (pneumoperitoneum plus Trendelenburg), less volume is delivered at the same set pressure, lowering tidal volume and risking hypoventilation and increased dead-space ventilation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Delivered tidal volume will stay constant while peak airway pressure rises sharply
choices.rationale: Incorrect. That describes volume-control behavior, where volume is fixed and pressure varies. In pressure control the pressure is capped, so volume, not pressure, changes when compliance falls.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Delivered tidal volume will increase because lower compliance raises driving pressure
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Lower compliance means less volume per unit of pressure. The driving pressure is set by the clinician and unchanged here, so volume falls rather than rises.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Peak airway pressure will exceed the set inspiratory pressure, causing barotrauma
choices.rationale: Incorrect. A defining feature of pressure control is that airway pressure is limited to the set value, which protects against barotrauma; the trade-off is variable (here reduced) tidal volume.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Minute ventilation will be unchanged because the ventilator compensates automatically
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Standard pressure-control mode does not auto-adjust to maintain a volume target (that is pressure-regulated volume control). With a fixed pressure and reduced compliance, tidal volume and minute ventilation fall.

questions.explanation: In pressure-control ventilation the clinician sets inspiratory pressure (and PEEP) and the resulting tidal volume depends on compliance and resistance. When compliance drops (pneumoperitoneum, steep Trendelenburg, obesity, ARDS) or resistance rises, delivered tidal volume falls at the same set pressure, risking hypoventilation and increased dead space. The advantage is a capped peak airway pressure that limits barotrauma. In volume control the reverse holds: tidal volume is fixed and airway pressure varies, so falling compliance raises peak pressures instead.

source.title: Pressure Controlled Ventilation - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason: URL loads and is public (StatPearls "Pressure Controlled Ventilation", NCBI Bookshelf NBK555897). It supports the answer: the article states that in pressure-control mode the delivered tidal volume varies with thoracic/lung compliance because inspiratory pressure is fixed, so a drop in compliance lowers delivered Vt. The medicine is correct and unambiguous. Pneumoperitoneum + steep Trendelenburg acutely reduce thoracopulmonary compliance; in PCV, Vt is the dependent variable (Vt ≈ compliance × driving pressure), so with pressure unchanged and compliance reduced, delivered Vt falls, risking hypoventilation — answer A. Distractors are correctly wrong: B (peak pressure is capped at the set value in PCV, so it does not rise sharply, and Vt does not stay constant), C (driving pressure is set, not raised by lower compliance; Vt falls), D (PCV caps pressure at the set value, so it cannot exceed it — barotrauma-by-pressure-overshoot is volume-control reasoning), E (standard PCV does not auto-compensate minute ventilation; that is a feature of adaptive/volume-targeted modes like PRVC). Rationales accurate. Approve for paid bank.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman receives a single-shot spinal anesthetic with hyperbaric bupivacaine plus 200 mcg of preservative-free morphine for a total hip arthroplasty. She is comfortable in the PACU and is transferred to a standard surgical ward. At approximately 10 hours after the spinal injection, the nurse finds her difficult to arouse with a respiratory rate of 6 breaths/min and an SpO2 of 84% on room air. Which property of intrathecal morphine best explains the timing of this event?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Its high lipid solubility causes rapid systemic absorption and early respiratory depression
choices.rationale: This describes lipophilic opioids such as fentanyl and sufentanil, which cause early (typically within the first hour) and usually mild, short-lived effects. Morphine is hydrophilic, not lipophilic, so this property does not explain a delayed event at 10 hours.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Its hydrophilicity causes slow rostral spread within the CSF, producing delayed respiratory depression hours after injection
choices.rationale: Correct. Morphine is highly hydrophilic, so it remains in the CSF and migrates rostrally over hours to reach the brainstem respiratory centers. StatPearls notes respiratory depression from intrathecal morphine typically occurs 6 to 18 hours after injection, matching this 10-hour presentation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It directly antagonizes peripheral chemoreceptors in the carotid body
choices.rationale: Opioid-induced respiratory depression is mediated centrally at brainstem respiratory centers (and blunting of the ventilatory response to CO2 and hypoxia), not by direct antagonism of carotid body chemoreceptors. This is not the mechanism behind the delayed timing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It triggers histamine release that causes bronchospasm and hypoxia
choices.rationale: Morphine can cause histamine release (e.g., pruritus), but the clinical picture here—somnolence with a markedly low respiratory rate—is central hypoventilation, not bronchospasm. Histamine release does not account for delayed central respiratory depression.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It undergoes rapid plasma reuptake, shortening its duration and limiting late effects
choices.rationale: Rapid plasma reuptake describes lipophilic opioids, which have short durations. Morphine's slow CSF clearance is exactly why its analgesia and respiratory risk are prolonged, making this the opposite of the correct explanation.

questions.explanation: Intrathecal opioid pharmacology is governed by lipid solubility. Hydrophilic morphine is poorly absorbed by spinal tissue, stays in the CSF, and spreads rostrally (cephalad) over many hours to reach the brainstem, producing delayed respiratory depression characteristically 6 to 18 hours after injection (earlier if systemic opioids are co-administered). Lipophilic agents (fentanyl, sufentanil) act early and briefly. This is why patients receiving intrathecal morphine require extended respiratory monitoring and caution with additional systemic opioids during the first 24 hours.

source.title: Spinal Opioids in Anesthetic Practice - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason:
URL loads, is public (NCBI Bookshelf, StatPearls "Neuraxial Opioids"), and directly supports the answer. The source states morphine's "hydrophilic nature enables slow rostral spread with prolonged analgesia" and that "respiratory depression from intrathecal morphine typically occurs 6 to 18 hours after injection" — the ~10-hour delayed event in the vignette falls squarely in this window, validating correct answer B (hydrophilicity → slow rostral CSF spread → delayed respiratory depression).

Medicine is correct and unambiguous. The clinical scenario is realistic: 200 mcg (0.2 mg) preservative-free intrathecal morphine is a standard dose for hip arthroplasty, and delayed respiratory depression 6-18h later is the classic, well-described risk requiring extended monitoring. The distractors are all accurately wrong: (A) inverts the physiology — lipophilic opioids like fentanyl cause EARLY depression via rapid uptake, not morphine; (C) morphine acts on central mu receptors/brainstem respiratory centers, not by antagonizing carotid chemoreceptors; (D) histamine release/bronchospasm does not explain a delayed sedation-plus-hypopnea presentation and is not the mechanism of opioid respiratory depression; (E) morphine's hydrophilicity causes the OPPOSITE — slow CSF clearance and prolonged, not shortened, effect. The correct answer and rationales are accurate and internally consistent.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man undergoing transurethral resection of the prostate receives a spinal anesthetic with hyperbaric bupivacaine to which 30 mcg of clonidine is added as an adjuvant. Compared with bupivacaine alone, which combination of effects is most consistent with the addition of intrathecal clonidine?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prolonged sensory and motor block with an increased risk of hypotension and bradycardia
choices.rationale: Correct. Clonidine is an alpha-2 adrenergic agonist that potentiates and prolongs sensory and motor blockade and postoperative analgesia when added intrathecally, and its sympatholytic action predisposes to hypotension, bradycardia, and sedation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Shortened duration of analgesia with reflex tachycardia
choices.rationale: This is the opposite of clonidine's effect. As an alpha-2 agonist it prolongs analgesia and tends to lower heart rate (bradycardia) via reduced sympathetic outflow rather than producing tachycardia.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Faster onset of block with marked hypertension
choices.rationale: Clonidine's central sympatholysis lowers blood pressure rather than causing hypertension, and its principal benefit is prolongation of block and analgesia rather than a faster onset.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pruritus and delayed respiratory depression typical of a neuraxial opioid
choices.rationale: Pruritus and delayed respiratory depression are characteristic of neuraxial opioids such as morphine, not of clonidine. Clonidine is not an opioid and does not produce opioid-mediated pruritus or mu-receptor respiratory depression.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Reduced sedation with improved respiratory drive
choices.rationale: Clonidine increases sedation through central alpha-2 effects rather than reducing it, so this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Clonidine is a selective alpha-2 adrenergic agonist used as a neuraxial adjuvant. Added intrathecally to local anesthetic, it significantly prolongs sensory and motor block and the duration of postoperative analgesia. Its dose-dependent sympatholytic effects produce the characteristic side-effect triad of hypotension, bradycardia, and sedation, which must be anticipated and monitored. Unlike neuraxial opioids, it does not cause pruritus or mu-mediated delayed respiratory depression.

source.title: Clonidine as an adjuvant to hyperbaric bupivacaine for spinal anesthesia in elderly patients undergoing lower limb orthopedic surgeries - PMC

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED — publish. Source URL (https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4024678/) loads, is public, and is directly on-topic: "Clonidine as an adjuvant to hyperbaric bupivacaine for spinal anesthesia in elderly patients." Medicine is correct and answer A is unambiguous.

Support for A (prolonged sensory+motor block, increased risk of hypotension/bradycardia): The article confirms clonidine significantly prolonged sensory regression and motor block and extended duration of analgesia. It also explicitly describes the hemodynamic class effects — "It produces hypotension by activation of postsynaptic alpha-2 adrenoceptors in the brain stem" and cites a 31.3% hypotension incidence with clonidine 15-150 mcg; bradycardia (HR<60) occurred in 30% of the 30 mcg group. So "increased risk of hypotension and bradycardia" reflects the recognized, dose-dependent class effect.

Nuance (does not block publishing): this particular small, low-dose (<1 mcg/kg) study found no statistically significant systolic BP difference vs control. But option A says "increased RISK of," which matches the established pharmacology the article itself describes, and the question asks what is "most consistent with" intrathecal clonidine in general.

Distractors all contain a frank pharmacologic error, leaving A as the only defensible answer:
- B wrong: clonidine prolongs (not shortens) analgesia and causes bradycardia (not reflex tachycardia).
- C wrong: causes hypotension (not hypertension); onset not markedly faster.
- D wrong: pruritus/delayed respiratory depression are neuraxial OPIOID effects; alpha-2 agonists are specifically not associated with these — good discriminating distractor.
- E wrong: clonidine causes sedation, not reduced sedation/improved respiratory drive.

Rationales are accurate and the keyed answer is correct.

questions.stem: A 54-year-old woman receives a right-sided thoracic paravertebral block at T4 for unilateral mastectomy. The thoracic paravertebral space communicates medially with the epidural space through the intervertebral foramen and is bounded anterolaterally by the parietal pleura. Given these anatomic relationships, which complication is most directly attributable to the boundaries of this space?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pneumothorax from advancing the needle through the parietal pleura
choices.rationale: Correct. The parietal pleura forms the anterolateral boundary of the paravertebral space, so a needle advanced too far anterolaterally can puncture the pleura and lung, producing pneumothorax—a complication directly tied to that boundary.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cauda equina syndrome from direct injection into the lumbar thecal sac
choices.rationale: A T4 thoracic block is far above the cauda equina, and the paravertebral space is not the lumbar thecal sac. While unintended intrathecal spread is possible, cauda equina syndrome is anatomically inconsistent with a high thoracic injection.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Vertebral artery injection causing immediate seizure
choices.rationale: The vertebral artery is a cervical/cranial structure traversing the transverse foramina of the cervical vertebrae, not a boundary of the thoracic paravertebral space, so this is not the relevant risk here.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Recurrent laryngeal nerve block causing hoarseness
choices.rationale: Recurrent laryngeal nerve involvement is a risk of neck blocks (e.g., interscalene), not a thoracic paravertebral block at T4, and is unrelated to this space's boundaries.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Phrenic nerve block causing hemidiaphragmatic paresis from cervical spread
choices.rationale: Phrenic nerve (C3-C5) involvement is characteristic of interscalene/supraclavicular blocks. While extensive cephalad spread is theoretically possible, it is not the complication most directly defined by the parietal-pleural boundary of the thoracic paravertebral space.

questions.explanation: The thoracic paravertebral space is a wedge-shaped space bounded anterolaterally by the parietal pleura, posteriorly by the superior costotransverse ligament, and medially by the vertebral body and intervertebral foramen (through which it communicates with the epidural space). Because the parietal pleura/lung lies immediately anterolateral, over-advancement of the needle risks pleural puncture and pneumothorax. The medial communication also explains the recognized risk of epidural and unintentional intrathecal spread, and diffusion can cause ipsilateral sympathetic effects (e.g., Horner syndrome) and hypotension.

source.title: Thoracic Paravertebral Block - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason: Source (StatPearls "Thoracic Paravertebral Block", NCBI Bookshelf NBK570560) is public and loaded successfully. It directly supports the stem and answer: parietal pleura is the anterolateral boundary, the space communicates medially with the epidural space via the intervertebral foramen, and pneumothorax is explicitly listed as a complication tied to parietal pleura injury from needle advancement. Answer A is correct and is the only option attributable to the boundaries described. Distractors are medically sound wrong answers: B (cauda equina/lumbar thecal sac) is anatomically impossible at a T4 thoracic block; C (vertebral artery) and D (recurrent laryngeal nerve) are cervical/mediastinal structures irrelevant to T4 paravertebral boundaries; E (phrenic/hemidiaphragmatic paresis from cervical spread) is plausible-sounding but not attributable to the stated boundaries, making A the clearly best answer. Medicine is accurate and unambiguous; rationales are correct.

questions.stem: While performing an ultrasound-guided interscalene block for shoulder surgery, you place a high-frequency linear probe transversely at the level of the cricoid cartilage and identify the brachial plexus as a vertical stack of two to three round hypoechoic structures (the 'stoplight' sign). Between which two structures do these nerve elements lie, and what is the most common complication of a successful block at this level?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Between the anterior and middle scalene muscles; hemidiaphragmatic paresis from phrenic nerve block
choices.rationale: Correct. At the interscalene groove the C5-C6 elements sit between the anterior and middle scalene muscles as the 'stoplight' sign. The phrenic nerve lies on the anterior scalene just anterior to the plexus, and local anesthetic spread reliably produces ipsilateral hemidiaphragmatic paresis—the most common complication.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Between the sternocleidomastoid and the internal jugular vein; pneumothorax
choices.rationale: The plexus at this level lies in the interscalene groove, not between the sternocleidomastoid and IJ. Pneumothorax is far more typical of the lower (supraclavicular/infraclavicular) approaches where the pleura is nearby, not the interscalene level.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Between the longus colli and the carotid artery; recurrent laryngeal nerve block
choices.rationale: These are not the sonographic landmarks for the interscalene plexus. Recurrent laryngeal nerve block and hoarseness can occur but are not the most common complication; hemidiaphragmatic paresis is.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Between the omohyoid and the subclavian artery; total spinal anesthesia
choices.rationale: The omohyoid and subclavian artery are landmarks more relevant to the supraclavicular region, not the interscalene groove. Total spinal is a rare catastrophic event, not the most common complication.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Within the prevertebral fascia anterior to the vertebral artery; Horner syndrome only
choices.rationale: The plexus is not anterior to the vertebral artery, and although Horner syndrome (stellate ganglion spread) can occur, it is not the most common complication. Hemidiaphragmatic paresis predominates.

questions.explanation: On transverse ultrasound at the cricoid level, the C5 and C6 roots appear as round hypoechoic structures stacked vertically in the groove between the anterior and middle scalene muscles—the classic 'stoplight' or 'traffic light' sign. Because the phrenic nerve runs on the anterior surface of the anterior scalene muscle immediately anterior to the plexus, local anesthetic predictably blocks it, causing ipsilateral hemidiaphragmatic paresis in nearly all patients. This is why the interscalene block is relatively contraindicated in patients with significant respiratory compromise.

source.title: Interscalene Block - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason: Source (StatPearls "Interscalene Block", NBK519491) loads, is public, and supports the question. It confirms the probe is placed at the level of the cricoid cartilage, that the brachial plexus lies "in between the anterior and middle scalene muscles," and describes the ultrasound appearance as "2 or 3 hollow circles ('stoplight')" — matching the stem precisely and validating correct answer A's anatomy. The source lists "ipsilateral phrenic nerve block and diaphragmatic hemiparesis" / "hemiparalysis of the diaphragm" as a complication; hemidiaphragmatic paresis from phrenic nerve block is the universally accepted MOST COMMON complication of interscalene block in anesthesiology (classically ~100% incidence with standard local anesthetic volumes), so A is medically correct and unambiguous. Distractors are all clearly wrong: B (SCM/IJV; pneumothorax is a supraclavicular risk, rare here), C (longus colli/carotid; RLN block — wrong location), D (omohyoid/subclavian; total spinal is rare, not most common), E (anterior to vertebral artery; Horner is a side effect but neither "only" nor most common). Single best answer is A. Rationales accurate.
